pages.title: Welcome to Classroom Communication & Data Safety
pages.subtitle: Learn how to communicate about students safely while protecting sensitive information.

video.caption: Introduction to Classroom Communication & Data Safety

pages.title: Why this matters in education
pages.text: Part of an educator’s job is communicating with parents, administrators, counselors, and other staff about students. These conversations often involve sensitive information, such as grades, behavior, accommodations, attendance, health-related details, or personal situations. Because of this, communication mistakes can accidentally expose private student information.

image.alt: Educator communicating with parents and staff

pages.title: Why communication mistakes matter
pages.text:
When sensitive student information is shared incorrectly, several problems can happen:
  • Student harm – private information may be exposed to people who should not see it
  • Loss of trust – families may lose confidence in the school’s ability to protect information
  • Policy or legal consequences – privacy rules such as FERPA exist to protect student records
Most privacy incidents in schools are not caused by hackers. They are often caused by everyday mistakes such as sending an email to the wrong person, sharing too much information, or using the wrong communication tool.

image.alt: Consequences of mishandling sensitive student information

pages.title: Use approved school communication channels
pages.text:
Student information should be shared only through approved school systems. Examples may include:
  • School email
  • Official parent communication platforms
  • Approved learning management systems
  • District-approved messaging tools
Avoid using personal email, informal group chats, social media, or unapproved apps for student-related communication unless school policy clearly allows it.

pages.title: Share only what is necessary
pages.text: Good communication does not mean sharing every detail. A useful rule is: share only the information needed for the situation. For example, a message may need to explain that a deadline changed, but it may not need to include a student's private health issue, family issue, or counseling detail. Sharing more than necessary increases privacy risk.

pages.title: Email and messaging risks
pages.text:
Email and messaging mistakes are common. Examples include:
  • Sending a message to the wrong parent or guardian
  • Replying all when sensitive information should be private
  • Using autocomplete and selecting the wrong recipient
  • Including student details in an informal chat
Before sending, pause and verify who will receive the message and whether all included information is truly necessary.

image.alt: Email and messaging mistakes involving student information

pages.title: Shared documents and permissions
pages.text:
Shared documents can also expose student information if permissions are not set correctly. Risks include:
  • Sharing a grade sheet with the wrong people
  • Leaving a document set to Anyone with the link can view
  • Including student names and private details in widely shared files
Always confirm who can open, view, or edit a document before sharing it.

image.alt: Shared document permissions and student data safety

pages.title: Casual communication can still create risk
pages.text:
Not every privacy incident happens in a formal report or official email. Risk can also come from:
  • Informal staff chats
  • Text messages
  • Personal notes shared too widely
  • Conversations in public areas
  • Posting classroom details online
Even if the intention is helpful, sensitive student information should not be casually shared where others may see or hear it.

image.alt: Casual communication risks in school settings

pages.title: What to do if you make a communication mistake
pages.text:
If you think student information was sent, posted, or shared incorrectly:
  1. Do not ignore it
  2. Stop further sharing if possible
  3. Report the issue according to school policy
  4. Notify the appropriate administrator, privacy contact, or designated staff member
Quick reporting helps the school respond appropriately and reduce further exposure.

image.alt: Reporting a student communication privacy mistake
